items.title: Anthropic splits Claude subscriptions on June 15 — programmatic use gets its own metered credit pool, and OpenClaw is back on the menu
items.summary: Anthropic announced via @ClaudeDevs on May 13-14 that paid Claude subscriptions will split into interactive and programmatic pools starting June 15. Agent SDK calls, `claude -p`, Claude Code GitHub Actions, and third-party agents like OpenClaw move from the existing subscription pool to a new dedicated monthly credit billed at API rates: Pro $20, Max 5x $100, Max 20x $200, Team Standard $20/seat, Team Premium $100/seat. The credit is non-rollover and once depleted, programmatic usage stops unless 'extra usage' billing is enabled (charged at standard API pay-as-you-go). Interactive Claude Code in the terminal is unaffected. The change reinstates OpenClaw and other third-party harnesses, which Anthropic banned in early April citing capacity issues and prompt-cache-hit-rate inefficiencies in third-party tools. Anthropic engineer Lydia Hallie clarified: 'You don't pay extra. It's the same subscription, same price per month.'
items.why_it_matters: For a Python developer using Claude Code, the Agent SDK, or any third-party harness (OpenClaw, NanoClaw), this is the operational change that matters most this quarter. The split means casual interactive coding from the terminal stays inside your subscription, but anything running headless — a GitHub Action, a scheduled agent, a script that pipes to `claude -p` — draws from the new $20-$200 credit at full API rates, and overruns no longer dip into your chat pool. Practical implication: if you've been using Claude Code's CLI for batch jobs without thinking about cost, those workloads get metered separately starting June 15. The reinstatement of OpenClaw is the other half of the story — Anthropic is now treating its subscription as two separate billing surfaces, which is how the third-party agent ecosystem can legally coexist with flat-rate consumer plans.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Claude paid plans split into interactive and programmatic pools on June 15 — Agent SDK, `claude -p`, GitHub Actions, and OpenClaw draw from a new $20-$200 metered credit at API rates.

takes.take: Reads the change as a quiet reversal of the April OpenClaw ban — third-party harnesses are back, but priced at their true API cost rather than subsidized by subscriptions.
Frames it as Claude subscriptions getting separate budgets for programmatic use, billed at full API prices.
Calls it putting Claude agents on a meter across subscriptions — the end of headless usage being implicitly free under Pro/Max.
Frames the change as Claude subscriptions no longer including Agent SDK and `claude -p` usage by default.

items.title: Mira Murati's Thinking Machines unveils TML-Interaction-Small — a 276B-parameter MoE that listens while it speaks
items.summary: Thinking Machines Lab — Mira Murati's startup founded in 2025 after her departure as OpenAI CTO — unveiled 'interaction models' on May 11, with the first preview release named TML-Interaction-Small: a 276B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts architecture with 12B active parameters that responds in roughly 0.40 seconds, the speed of natural human conversation. The model breaks communication into ~200ms micro-turns and uses a two-part architecture: an Interaction Model that stays in constant exchange with the user (dialog management, presence, immediate follow-ups), backed by a Background Model that handles sustained reasoning, web browsing, and complex tool calls asynchronously and streams results back. The lab is positioning this as a replacement for OpenAI Realtime-style turn-based voice agents — the system is full-duplex, meaning it can listen and speak simultaneously rather than waiting for the user to finish. Available now as a research preview to select partners, with broader release later in the year.
items.why_it_matters: This is the first product Thinking Machines has shipped, and the architectural choice is more interesting than any single demo: most 'realtime voice' models today are still polite turn-takers, while interaction models target the actual mechanics of human conversation (interrupting, backchannel, overlapping speech). For an HTML/CSS/Python developer, the practical hook is what changes if voice-first agents become genuinely conversational — the API surface shifts from synchronous request/response to a duplex stream, which is structurally different from how you'd wire up a chatbot today. Worth tracking as a direct contender to OpenAI's GPT-Realtime-2 (released May 7) and Google's Gemini Live family for the 'agent that actually holds a conversation' use case.
items.why_it_matters_brief: TML-Interaction-Small responds in 0.40 seconds and runs full-duplex audio — Mira Murati's lab is betting voice agents need a different architecture from turn-based chat APIs.

takes.take: Frames the preview as near-realtime voice and video conversation — emphasizes the demo over the architecture, but notes the move beyond OpenAI Realtime's turn-based design.
Calls the model highly responsive and humanlike — positions it as the first model explicitly designed for the conversational-presence use case.
Reads the release as a native multimodal architecture for real-time human-AI collaboration — multimodality is structural here, not bolted on.
Pulls out the 276B/12B-active MoE configuration and the simultaneous listen-and-talk capability as the technical headlines.

items.title: Cerebras debuts on Nasdaq today at $185 a share — $5.55B raised, $56.4B valuation, the biggest AI hardware IPO of 2026
items.summary: Cerebras Systems, the wafer-scale AI chip company, priced its IPO at $185 a share late on May 13 — above the marketed $150-160 range — raising $5.55 billion by selling 30 million shares and pinning a fully diluted valuation of $56.4 billion. Trading opens on Nasdaq under ticker CBRS on May 14 (today), closing May 15. The price walk tells the story of book demand: on May 4 the company filed at $115-125 for 28 million shares, then bumped to $150-160 and 30 million a week later, then priced above that. Lead underwriters: Morgan Stanley, Citigroup, Barclays, and UBS. Cerebras' previous IPO attempt in September 2024 stalled over questions about its concentration of revenue from G42, the Microsoft-backed UAE customer; the renewed bid arrives with broader infrastructure deals as backdrop, including the SpaceX/Colossus 1 compute build and the recent NVIDIA-Corning and IREN partnerships totaling more than $5 billion this week.
items.why_it_matters: The headline number is the alternative to Nvidia getting funded by public-market investors at scale — $56.4 billion is a real signal that the AI compute market wants more than one supplier. Worth watching the open today for the Nvidia delta: Cerebras' wafer-scale design (one giant chip vs. many GPUs) is the architecturally different bet, and CBRS will trade as a referendum on whether anti-Nvidia alternatives can hold premium multiples. For Gabe: this is also a marker of where AI capex is going — public-market dollars now follow the chip-supply story as closely as the model-output story, and 'is there a Nvidia alternative?' has gone from a research question to a stock you can buy.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Cerebras prices its IPO above range at $185 — the wafer-scale AI chip company opens on Nasdaq today at a $56.4B valuation, the biggest AI hardware listing of 2026.

takes.take: First to report the $185 pricing above the marketed range — confirms the book closed over-subscribed.
Confirms the May 14 Nasdaq debut and May 15 settlement; lists the underwriter syndicate.
Pegs CBRS as 2026's biggest debut by total raise — frames the listing as the year's headline AI-hardware market test.

items.title: Google says it caught the first AI-built zero-day exploit before it shipped — a 2FA bypass developed with an AI model, planned for 'mass exploitation'
items.summary: Google's Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) said on May 11 it has 'high confidence' that a criminal threat actor used an AI model to develop a zero-day exploit — a Python script bypassing two-factor authentication on a popular open-source web-based system administration tool. GTIG says it intercepted the campaign before the actor could deploy it in a 'mass exploitation event.' This is the first publicly disclosed zero-day GTIG attributes to AI-assisted vulnerability discovery. The full report also describes North Korean APT45 running AI-driven exploit validation against thousands of software flaws, and China-linked groups showing 'significant interest' in AI for vulnerability discovery. GTIG chief analyst John Hultquist: 'There's a misconception that the AI vulnerability race is imminent. The reality is that it's already begun.'
items.why_it_matters: The shift from 'AI will eventually be used to find software flaws' to 'AI just found and weaponized a zero-day in production' is the real headline. For anyone running Python services (Gabe writes Python), the practical question is no longer whether attackers will use AI to scan dependencies — they already are. The reported exploit targeted an open-source admin tool, which is exactly the kind of long-tail dependency most projects pull in without thinking. Pair this with Anthropic's Project Glasswing and OpenAI's Daybreak: the major labs are positioning the defensive use of frontier-capable AI as the counter to exactly this offensive use, and the next 12 months are likely to be the first real test of whether defense scales as fast as offense.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Google's threat team caught a criminal group deploying an AI-built 2FA-bypass zero-day before it hit production — the 'AI finds vulnerabilities' arms race isn't future tense anymore.

takes.take: Frames the disclosure as 'first AI-built zero-day exploit in cyberattack' — leans on the firstness of the public attribution.
Describes the planned 'mass hack spree' as the operational objective — useful framing for how the attackers planned to deploy at scale.
Notes the 2FA-bypass target specifically and connects AI-assisted discovery to APT45 and Chinese groups as a broader pattern.
Frames Google as having 'thwarted' the campaign — interesting given how rare it is for a vendor to publicly claim a pre-deployment intercept.

items.title: US clears H200 exports to Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance and seven more — but no chips have shipped, and Beijing wants the orders blocked
items.summary: The US Commerce Department has approved roughly 10 Chinese companies — including Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance and JD.com, plus distributors Lenovo and Foxconn — to purchase Nvidia H200 chips, Reuters reported on May 14. Each approved customer can buy up to 75,000 chips per the licensing terms. Despite the clearance, no deliveries have been made: Chinese firms pulled back after guidance from Beijing, and there is pressure in China to block or tightly vet the orders. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, who was not initially on the White House delegation to Beijing this week, joined the trip after a Trump invitation — Trump reportedly picked Huang up in Alaska en route to a summit with Xi. The China-Nvidia revenue line has been near-zero for two years since the prior H100/H800 export controls; H200 (Hopper-generation, 141GB HBM3e) is the highest-spec accelerator the US has permitted for China to date.
items.why_it_matters: This is the clearest signal yet of where the AI export-controls regime is settling — not 'no Nvidia in China' but a licensed-and-counted channel for second-tier hardware. The lack of actual shipments matters more than the clearance: Beijing's reluctance shows the policy game is now bidirectional, with both governments using AI compute as a strategic lever. The DeepSeek raise covered in this morning's am file fits the same picture — China is building toward sovereign AI compute (Huawei Ascend, Cambricon) precisely because dependence on Nvidia exports is now considered a national vulnerability rather than a commercial detail.
items.why_it_matters_brief: US licenses Nvidia H200 sales to Alibaba/Tencent/ByteDance and seven more (75k chips each) — but Beijing has frozen the orders, and Huang is now physically in China trying to break the standoff.

takes.take: Headlines the contradiction directly — 'US clears H200 sales to 10 Chinese firms, but not a single chip has shipped' — and tracks Huang's Beijing trip as the operational fix.
Confirms the 10-firm scope and the 75k-chip-per-customer cap as the licensing parameters.
Reports the Trump-Huang Alaska pickup and the unscheduled addition to the White House delegation — context for how the trip materialized.

items.title: Visual Studio 2026 May update ships Copilot agent skill auto-discovery and dedicated C# and C++ agents — Microsoft picks up the same skill model as Claude
items.summary: Microsoft released the Visual Studio 2026 May update on May 12, headlining auto-discovery of 'Copilot agent skills' — reusable instruction sets that teach the IDE's agent how to handle specific tasks like running a build pipeline, generating boilerplate, or following a team's coding standards. The release adds dedicated C# and C++ agents and a sparkle-comment button in the Git Changes window that suggests fixes for bugs and performance issues in local diffs before commit. Visual Studio 2026 went GA in November 2025 with deep Copilot integration as its tentpole feature; this is the first major update since GA and signals where the IDE roadmap is going: skills as the unit of automation, agents per language ecosystem, and AI review baked into the commit flow rather than relegated to a PR comment.
items.why_it_matters: The 'skill' abstraction is now showing up everywhere — Claude Code's skills landed last quarter, Claude for Small Business ships 15 prebuilt skills (covered in this morning's am file), and now Visual Studio adopts the same pattern as the unit of agent automation. For Gabe, the broader trend worth tracking is convergence: the industry seems to have settled on 'agent + skill + tool connector' as the canonical agent architecture regardless of which IDE or which lab. If you build a workflow today and want it to plug into both Claude Code and VS 2026, exposing it as a discoverable skill is now the path that minimizes lock-in. C# and C++ agents are also a fair entry point if you've wanted to peek outside Python — VS 2026's free Community edition still ships full Copilot integration.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Visual Studio 2026's May update lands Copilot agent skill auto-discovery and per-language agents — the same 'skill' abstraction now spans Claude Code, Claude for Small Business, and VS.

takes.take: Documents the skill auto-discovery API and the Git Changes sparkle-comment fix-suggestion flow as the headline changes for May.
Frames the update as 'how AI is transforming the way developers code' — useful walk-through of the agent-skill workflow inside a typical .NET project.

items.title: Nvidia partners with David Silver's Ineffable Intelligence to build RL-first compute — pure reinforcement learning is back as a frontier bet
items.summary: Nvidia announced on May 13 a deep engineering partnership with Ineffable Intelligence, the London startup founded in late 2025 by UCL professor David Silver — former head of DeepMind's reinforcement-learning team and lead architect of AlphaGo and AlphaZero. The collaboration will build infrastructure for large-scale reinforcement learning: data pipelines, interconnect, memory bandwidth, and serving topologies optimized for the tight act-observe-score-update loops RL workloads generate on the fly, rather than the static-dataset throughput pretraining demands. The hardware: Nvidia's current Grace Blackwell and forthcoming Vera Rubin platforms. Ineffable raised a record $1.1B seed in April at a $5.1B valuation, co-led by Sequoia, Lightspeed and Nvidia — the largest seed round in European tech history. The technical bet is that frontier capability will plateau under 'all the human text on the internet' pretraining and that learn-from-experience RL is the way through the data wall.
items.why_it_matters: For two years the frontier story has been 'scale pretraining → scale RLHF on top.' Ineffable's bet is more radical: skip the pretraining-as-bottleneck and put reinforcement learning at the center. That this is being co-engineered with Nvidia means there's a real possibility the next generation of frontier hardware (Rubin) is shaped by RL workloads as much as by transformer pretraining. For developers, the practical question is whether self-play and agent-experience pipelines start showing up as first-class primitives in PyTorch/TensorFlow analogs over the next 12-18 months. Silver's AlphaZero pedigree is the credibility — this is the team that already proved pure RL can beat human-data-bootstrapped systems in a bounded domain.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Nvidia is co-engineering RL-specific infrastructure with David Silver's Ineffable Intelligence at $5.1B — a bet that learning-from-experience replaces 'scrape all human text' as the next-gen training paradigm.

takes.take: Frames the deal as Jensen Huang 'betting on this British startup to build the next frontier of AI' — positions Ineffable as the European hedge in Nvidia's frontier-lab portfolio.
Pegs the Sequoia/Nvidia/Lightspeed seed at $5.1B valuation and notes Ineffable is Europe's largest-ever seed round.
Calls the joint effort 'breaching the data wall' — the strategic framing Ineffable itself uses for why RL-first matters now.
